packs.name: Splunk
packs.description: Protects against destructive Splunk CLI/API operations like index removal and REST API DELETE calls

destructive_patterns.name: splunk-remove-index
destructive_patterns.reason: splunk remove index deletes an index and its data permanently.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
Removing a Splunk index permanently deletes all indexed data within it. Historical logs, events, and metrics are irretrievably lost. Any searches, dashboards, or alerts referencing this index will fail.

Safer alternatives:
- splunk list index: Review index before removal
- Archive the data before deletion
- Use retention policies instead of manual deletion

destructive_patterns.name: splunk-clean-eventdata
destructive_patterns.reason: splunk clean eventdata permanently deletes indexed data.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
Clean eventdata permanently removes all events from the specified index. This cannot be undone. Use this only when you're certain the data is no longer needed.

Safer alternatives:
- Set retention policies for automatic cleanup
- Export data before cleaning
- Use splunk search to verify what will be deleted

destructive_patterns.name: splunk-delete-user-role
destructive_patterns.reason: splunk delete user/role removes access configurations. Verify before deleting.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
Deleting a user removes their access and any saved searches or dashboards owned by them. Deleting a role affects all users assigned to it, potentially breaking access controls.

Safer alternatives:
- splunk list user/role: Review before deletion
- Disable the user instead of deleting
- Reassign role capabilities before deletion

destructive_patterns.name: splunk-api-delete
destructive_patterns.reason: Splunk REST DELETE calls can permanently remove objects. Verify the endpoint.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
Direct API DELETE calls to Splunk services can remove indexes, saved searches, dashboards, alerts, and other objects without confirmation.

Safer alternatives:
- GET the resource first to verify the object
- Use Splunk CLI or web UI for better feedback
- Export configuration before deletion

packs.name: Datadog
packs.description: Protects against destructive Datadog CLI/API operations like deleting monitors and dashboards.

destructive_patterns.name: datadog-ci-monitors-delete
destructive_patterns.reason: datadog-ci monitors delete removes a Datadog monitor.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
Deleting a Datadog monitor stops all alerting for that check. You will no longer be notified if the monitored condition occurs, potentially missing critical production issues.

Safer alternatives:
- datadog-ci monitors get <id>: Review the monitor configuration first
- Mute the monitor temporarily instead of deleting
- Export monitor JSON configuration as backup before deletion

destructive_patterns.name: datadog-ci-dashboards-delete
destructive_patterns.reason: datadog-ci dashboards delete removes a Datadog dashboard.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
Deleting a dashboard removes all widgets, queries, and layout configuration. Team members relying on this dashboard for visibility will lose access immediately.

Safer alternatives:
- datadog-ci dashboards get <id>: Export dashboard JSON first
- Clone the dashboard before making changes
- Use Terraform or Pulumi for version-controlled dashboard definitions

destructive_patterns.name: datadog-api-delete
destructive_patterns.reason: Datadog API DELETE calls remove monitors/dashboards/synthetics.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
Direct API DELETE calls permanently remove Datadog resources without confirmation prompts. Monitors, dashboards, and synthetic tests are deleted immediately.

Safer alternatives:
- GET the resource first to verify the ID and export configuration
- Use datadog-ci CLI which provides better feedback
- Use Terraform/Pulumi for auditable, reversible infrastructure changes

destructive_patterns.name: terraform-datadog-destroy
destructive_patterns.reason: terraform destroy targeting Datadog resources removes monitoring infrastructure.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
Terraform destroy removes Datadog monitors, dashboards, and other resources defined in your configuration. While Terraform tracks state, the actual monitoring resources are deleted immediately from Datadog.

Safer alternatives:
- terraform plan -destroy -target=... to preview deletions
- terraform state rm to stop managing without deleting
- Remove from Terraform config and apply instead of destroy

packs.name: PagerDuty
packs.description: Protects against destructive PagerDuty CLI/API operations like deleting services and schedules (which can break incident routing).

destructive_patterns.name: pd-service-delete
destructive_patterns.reason: pd service delete removes a PagerDuty service, which can break incident routing.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
Deleting a PagerDuty service removes all incident routing, integrations, and escalation policies attached to it. Incidents will no longer be created for this service, potentially causing outages to go unnoticed.

Safer alternatives:
- pd service list: Verify the service before deletion
- Disable the service temporarily instead of deleting
- Export service configuration via API before deletion

destructive_patterns.name: pd-schedule-delete
destructive_patterns.reason: pd schedule delete removes a PagerDuty schedule.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
Deleting a schedule removes all on-call rotations and overrides. Escalation policies using this schedule will have gaps in coverage, potentially leaving incidents unassigned.

Safer alternatives:
- pd schedule get <id>: Review schedule details first
- Update the schedule rather than deleting
- Check which escalation policies reference this schedule

destructive_patterns.name: pd-escalation-policy-delete
destructive_patterns.reason: pd escalation-policy delete removes a PagerDuty escalation policy.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
Deleting an escalation policy breaks incident routing for all services using it. Incidents may not be escalated properly, leading to delayed response times.

Safer alternatives:
- Review which services use this escalation policy
- Update the policy rather than deleting
- Assign services to a different policy before deletion

destructive_patterns.name: pd-user-delete
destructive_patterns.reason: pd user delete removes a PagerDuty user.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
Deleting a user removes them from all schedules and escalation policies. On-call coverage may have gaps, and incident history associated with this user may be affected.

Safer alternatives:
- Deactivate the user instead of deleting
- Reassign their schedule shifts first
- Transfer incident ownership before deletion

destructive_patterns.name: pd-team-delete
destructive_patterns.reason: pd team delete removes a PagerDuty team.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
Deleting a team removes the organizational grouping and may affect access controls, service ownership, and escalation policies.

Safer alternatives:
- pd team list: Verify the team before deletion
- Reassign team members and services first
- Merge teams rather than delete

destructive_patterns.name: pagerduty-api-delete-service
destructive_patterns.reason: PagerDuty API DELETE /services/{id} deletes a PagerDuty service.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
Direct API deletion of a service removes all incident routing immediately. There is no confirmation prompt when using curl directly.

Safer alternatives:
- GET the service first to verify the ID
- Use the pd CLI for better feedback
- Export service configuration before deletion

destructive_patterns.name: pagerduty-api-delete-schedule
destructive_patterns.reason: PagerDuty API DELETE /schedules/{id} deletes a PagerDuty schedule.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
API deletion of a schedule removes on-call rotations immediately. Escalation policies referencing this schedule will have coverage gaps.

Safer alternatives:
- GET the schedule first to verify the ID
- Use the pd CLI for better feedback
- Check dependent escalation policies before deletion

packs.name: New Relic
packs.description: Protects against destructive New Relic CLI/API operations like deleting entities or alerting resources.

destructive_patterns.name: newrelic-entity-delete
destructive_patterns.reason: newrelic entity delete removes a New Relic entity, impacting observability.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
Deleting a New Relic entity removes all associated telemetry data, relationships, and alert configurations. Historical metrics for this entity will no longer be accessible.

Safer alternatives:
- newrelic entity search: Find and verify the entity first
- Tag the entity as deprecated instead of deleting
- Export entity configuration before deletion

destructive_patterns.name: newrelic-apm-app-delete
destructive_patterns.reason: newrelic apm application delete removes an APM application.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
Deleting an APM application removes all application performance data, traces, and associated alert policies. You lose visibility into application behavior and historical performance trends.

Safer alternatives:
- newrelic apm application get <id>: Review application details first
- Disable the APM agent in your application instead
- Export application settings and dashboards as backup

destructive_patterns.name: newrelic-workload-delete
destructive_patterns.reason: newrelic workload delete removes a workload definition.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
Deleting a workload removes the logical grouping of entities and any associated health status calculations. Teams using this workload for service overview will lose their aggregated view.

Safer alternatives:
- Review workload entities and dependencies first
- Export workload definition using the API
- Modify the workload rather than deleting and recreating

destructive_patterns.name: newrelic-synthetics-delete
destructive_patterns.reason: newrelic synthetics delete removes a synthetics monitor.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
Deleting a synthetics monitor stops all uptime and availability checking for the monitored endpoint. You will no longer receive alerts when the endpoint becomes unavailable or slow.

Safer alternatives:
- Disable the monitor temporarily instead of deleting
- Export monitor configuration as code (Terraform/Pulumi)
- newrelic synthetics search: Verify you're deleting the correct monitor

destructive_patterns.name: newrelic-api-delete
destructive_patterns.reason: New Relic API DELETE calls remove monitoring/alerting resources.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
Direct API DELETE calls permanently remove New Relic resources without confirmation. Alert policies, dashboards, and monitors are deleted immediately.

Safer alternatives:
- GET the resource first to verify the ID and export configuration
- Use the New Relic CLI for better feedback and confirmation
- Use Terraform/Pulumi for version-controlled resource management

destructive_patterns.name: newrelic-graphql-delete-mutation
destructive_patterns.reason: New Relic GraphQL delete mutations can remove monitoring resources.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
GraphQL delete mutations remove New Relic resources via the NerdGraph API. This affects entities, dashboards, alert policies, and other observability resources.

Safer alternatives:
- Run a query first to verify the GUID or resource ID
- Use the New Relic CLI or UI for deletion with better feedback
- Export the resource definition before deletion

packs.name: Prometheus/Grafana
packs.description: Protects against destructive Prometheus/Grafana operations like deleting time series data or dashboards/datasources.

destructive_patterns.name: prometheus-rules-file-delete
destructive_patterns.reason: Deleting Prometheus rule/config files can break alerting and monitoring coverage.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
Deleting Prometheus configuration or rule files stops alerting for the defined conditions. After Prometheus reloads, those alerts will no longer fire, potentially leaving critical conditions unmonitored.

Safer alternatives:
- promtool check rules: Validate rule files before changes
- Back up files before deletion
- Use version control (git) for rule files

destructive_patterns.name: prometheus-tsdb-delete-series
destructive_patterns.reason: Prometheus TSDB delete_series permanently deletes time series data.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
The delete_series admin endpoint permanently removes time series data matching the specified selectors. This data cannot be recovered. Historical metrics for the deleted series will show gaps.

Safer alternatives:
- Query the series first to verify what will be deleted
- Use retention policies instead of manual deletion
- Take a TSDB snapshot before deletion

destructive_patterns.name: kubectl-delete-prometheus-operator-resources
destructive_patterns.reason: kubectl delete of Prometheus Operator resources (PrometheusRule/ServiceMonitor/PodMonitor) removes alerting/target configuration.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
Deleting Prometheus Operator CRDs removes alerting rules or scrape targets. The Prometheus Operator will update Prometheus configuration, potentially leaving services unmonitored.

Safer alternatives:
- kubectl get to review the resource first
- kubectl describe to see what it configures
- Export YAML before deletion: kubectl get -o yaml

destructive_patterns.name: grafana-cli-plugins-uninstall
destructive_patterns.reason: grafana-cli plugins uninstall removes a Grafana plugin, potentially breaking dashboards.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
Uninstalling a Grafana plugin breaks all dashboards using that plugin's panels or datasources. Users will see error messages where those panels were.

Safer alternatives:
- grafana-cli plugins list: Review installed plugins first
- Check which dashboards use the plugin before removal
- Update the plugin instead of uninstalling

destructive_patterns.name: grafana-api-delete-dashboard
destructive_patterns.reason: Grafana API DELETE /api/dashboards/... deletes dashboards.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
Deleting a Grafana dashboard removes all panels, queries, and configuration. Teams relying on this dashboard for monitoring will lose visibility.

Safer alternatives:
- GET /api/dashboards/uid/<uid> to export JSON first
- Use Grafana provisioning for version-controlled dashboards
- Use dashboard versioning in Grafana to restore later

destructive_patterns.name: grafana-api-delete-datasource
destructive_patterns.reason: Grafana API DELETE /api/datasources/... deletes datasources.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
Deleting a datasource breaks all dashboards and alerts using it. Panels will show 'Data source not found' errors, and alerting queries will fail.

Safer alternatives:
- GET the datasource first to verify the ID
- Check which dashboards use this datasource
- Use Grafana provisioning for datasource-as-code

destructive_patterns.name: grafana-api-delete-alert-notification
destructive_patterns.reason: Grafana API DELETE /api/alert-notifications/... deletes alert notification channels.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
Deleting a notification channel stops alert delivery to that destination. Alerts using this channel will fire but notifications won't be sent.

Safer alternatives:
- GET the notification channel first to verify the ID
- Check which alerts use this notification channel
- Disable the channel instead of deleting
